characters.name: Jordan Belfort
motivations: prove himself superior to the establishment
be worshipped by those around him

relationships.detail: Donnie is Jordan's most loyal lieutenant, amplifying his worst impulses
Naomi is both trophy and tether — represents the lifestyle Jordan craves and the family he neglects
Agent Denham is the incorruptible force Jordan cannot buy, charm, or outrun
Hanna introduces Jordan to the Wall Street culture of excess and amorality
Max serves as Jordan's conscience, horrified by the scale of corruption but unable to stop it

characters.name: Donnie Azoff
motivations: share in Jordan's wealth and power

relationships.detail: Quits his job on the spot to follow Jordan, becoming his most devoted disciple
Provokes Denham with petty taunts that accelerate the investigation

characters.name: Naomi Lapaglia
motivations: protect her children from Jordan's chaos
maintain her own power within the marriage

relationships.detail: Initially dazzled by Jordan's wealth, eventually repelled by his addiction and violence

characters.name: Agent Patrick Denham
motivations: prove that wealth does not place anyone above the law
resist the seduction of Jordan's lifestyle

relationships.detail: Refuses Jordan's attempted bribe on the yacht, becoming his implacable pursuer

characters.name: Mark Hanna
motivations: enjoy the spoils of Wall Street without guilt
initiate newcomers into the true culture of finance

relationships.detail: Teaches Jordan the foundational philosophy: the client's money is an illusion, cocaine keeps you sharp, and the game is rigged in your favor

characters.name: Max Belfort
motivations: protect his son from legal consequences
maintain some moral boundary within the operation

relationships.detail: Nicknamed 'Mad Max' for his temper but serves as the only voice of restraint Jordan occasionally heeds

places.name: Jordan's Queens Apartment
places.description: The modest apartment where Jordan and his first wife Teresa live at the start of his career. Small rooms, cheap furniture, a life of middle-class striving that Jordan finds suffocating and intolerable.

places.name: Stratton Oakmont Offices
places.description: The Long Island boiler room that grows into a sprawling empire of phone banks, trading floors, and drug-fueled debauchery. A kingdom of excess where Jordan rules as absolute monarch and the sales floor doubles as a circus.

places.name: Old Brookville Mansion
places.description: Jordan and Naomi's palatial Long Island estate with its white columns, swimming pool, and helicopter pad. The physical manifestation of everything Jordan has built — and everything he stands to lose.

places.name: Federal Prison Camp
places.description: The minimum-security federal prison where Jordan serves 22 months after his conviction. A stark, mundane environment that strips away every trapping of his former life, reducing the Wolf of Wall Street to inmate number and tennis partner.

objects.name: Quaaludes (Lemmon 714s)
objects.description: The rare vintage Quaaludes that Jordan and Donnie hoard and consume in escalating quantities. The Lemmon 714 scene — where Jordan crawls to his Lamborghini unable to walk or speak — is the film's most explicit depiction of excess tipping into self-destruction.
objects.significance: Track the arc from recreational indulgence to total loss of control. The drugs that make Jordan feel invincible ultimately render him literally unable to function, a physical metaphor for his entire trajectory.
rules: Consumption escalates in proportion to Jordan's power and paranoia
The country club Quaalude scene marks the point where excess becomes grotesque rather than glamorous

objects.name: The Naomi (Motor Yacht)
objects.description: Jordan's 170-foot yacht, named after his wife, which sinks in a Mediterranean storm when Jordan insists on sailing to Switzerland to hide money despite gale warnings. The sinking is both literal and symbolic.
objects.significance: Represents the peak of Jordan's material achievement and the moment nature itself rebels against his hubris. His refusal to heed warnings about the storm mirrors his refusal to heed warnings about the FBI investigation.
rules: Named after Naomi, tying his marriage to his wealth
The sinking forces Jordan to accept he cannot control everything through sheer will

objects.name: The Microphone / Sales Floor Podium
objects.description: The microphone and elevated platform from which Jordan delivers his legendary motivational speeches to the Stratton Oakmont sales floor, whipping hundreds of brokers into a frenzy of greed and loyalty.
objects.significance: Jordan's true superpower is his voice — his ability to sell anything to anyone. The microphone amplifies this gift. The film bookends with Jordan at a podium: first commanding his empire, finally reduced to teaching sales seminars to strangers in a hotel ballroom.
rules: The podium speeches mark key inflection points in the rise and fall
The final seminar scene mirrors the Stratton speeches but strips away the power and stakes

moments.label: A Kid from the Bronx
moments.description: Jordan Belfort arrives on Wall Street as a young, eager stockbroker with a freshly minted Series 7 license and a modest apartment in Queens. He is hungry, ambitious, and naive — a working-class kid who believes that hard work and talent will earn him a place at the table. His first wife Teresa supports him. He has no connections, no money, and no idea how the game actually works.

character_states.char_jordan: ambitious, naive, eager to prove himself, living modestly in Queens
character_states.char_max: proud of his son's legitimate Wall Street job, cautiously optimistic

transitions.description: Jordan enters the world of Wall Street finance, bringing raw charisma and hunger but no understanding of its true culture

moments.label: The Lunch with Hanna
moments.description: On his first day at L.F. Rothschild, senior broker Mark Hanna takes Jordan to lunch and reveals Wall Street's true philosophy: the client's money was never real, cocaine is a performance enhancer, and the only goal is to move money from the client's pocket to yours. Black Monday wipes out Jordan's firm, but Hanna's lessons survive. Jordan discovers penny stocks on Long Island, where his polished sales technique earns 50% commissions instead of 1%. He realizes he has found his angle.

character_states.char_jordan: absorbing Hanna's philosophy, losing his naivete, discovering his gift for sales
character_states.char_hanna: casually corrupting, treating amorality as simple professional wisdom

transitions.description: Internalizes Hanna's nihilistic philosophy — the money is not real, the game is rigged, and guilt is for amateurs
Chooses to exploit penny stock commissions using his Rothschild-trained sales skills, finding the gap between respectability and profitability

moments.label: Building the Empire
moments.description: Jordan founds Stratton Oakmont with Donnie Azoff and a crew of untrained, hungry outsiders. He teaches them his sales script — the 'straight line' method — and transforms a garage operation into a Wall Street powerhouse. The firm goes from penny stocks to IPOs. Money pours in. Jordan buys a mansion, a Ferrari, a helicopter. He meets Naomi at a party and begins an affair. The sales floor becomes a Roman circus of drugs, prostitutes, midget-tossing, and millions in daily commissions.

character_states.char_jordan: ascendant, intoxicated by power, building his kingdom with evangelical fervor
character_states.char_donnie: euphoric, devoted, feeding off Jordan's energy and largesse
character_states.char_naomi: attracted to Jordan's power and confidence, beginning the affair that will define her life

transitions.target: wealth, power, and a loyal army
transitions.description: Builds Stratton Oakmont from nothing into a firm generating millions per week through aggressive penny stock manipulation and IPO fraud
Donnie quits his job on the spot after seeing Jordan's pay stub, becoming his most loyal confederate and enabler
Begins the affair with Naomi that will destroy his first marriage and become the centerpiece of his lifestyle

moments.label: The Wolf at His Zenith
moments.description: Jordan is earning $49 million a year, married to Naomi, living in a mansion with a private helicopter and yacht. Forbes publishes a hit piece calling him 'The Wolf of Wall Street' — he frames it. His speeches to the sales floor are messianic. He throws legendary parties. He has Swiss bank accounts, a fleet of luxury cars, and the absolute loyalty of hundreds of brokers who worship him. He believes he is untouchable.

character_states.char_jordan: godlike, addicted to excess, convinced of his own invincibility
character_states.char_naomi: enjoying the lifestyle but beginning to see the cracks in Jordan's behavior
character_states.char_donnie: living in Jordan's reflected glory, escalating his own drug use
character_states.char_max: deeply uncomfortable with the scale of illegality, acting as reluctant accountant

transitions.target: the Forbes profile and the 'Wolf' identity
transitions.description: Embraces the Forbes attack piece as a badge of honor, cementing his public persona and attracting both recruits and federal attention
His reaction to the Forbes piece — pride instead of caution — reveals that Jordan's ego has overtaken his survival instincts

moments.label: The FBI Comes Calling
moments.description: Agent Patrick Denham boards Jordan's yacht under the guise of a friendly visit. Jordan, unable to resist showing off, attempts to bribe him with a job offer. Denham refuses with quiet contempt and leaves. The investigation accelerates. Jordan's drug use spirals — the Quaalude binge at the country club leaves him crawling on the ground, unable to walk, crashing his Lamborghini. Naomi grows increasingly hostile. The cracks are no longer hairline; they are structural.

character_states.char_jordan: rattled beneath the bravado, self-medicating with escalating drug binges
character_states.char_denham: patient, methodical, building an airtight case piece by piece
character_states.char_naomi: disgusted by Jordan's addiction, protective of their children, considering divorce
character_states.char_donnie: panicking, making reckless moves that create additional legal exposure

transitions.description: The failed bribe attempt on the yacht reveals Jordan's fundamental misunderstanding — he believes everyone has a price, but Denham does not
The Quaalude overdose at the country club strips Jordan of basic motor function, a physical manifestation of the control he is losing in every dimension of his life

moments.label: Running the Money
moments.description: Jordan attempts to smuggle cash to Swiss bank accounts using Naomi's aunt's name, strapping money to mules and sailing his yacht across the Mediterranean. When Swiss banking secrecy laws change, he pivots frantically. The yacht sinks in a storm after Jordan refuses to heed weather warnings — they are rescued by the Italian coast guard. Back home, Jordan discovers the FBI has flipped key associates. He delivers one final, desperate speech to the Stratton sales floor, attempting to rally his troops, but the walls are closing in from every direction.

character_states.char_jordan: desperate, scrambling, making increasingly reckless decisions to preserve his empire
character_states.char_donnie: terrified of prison, becoming a liability
character_states.char_naomi: demanding divorce, using the children as leverage
character_states.char_max: begging Jordan to take the deal and cooperate

transitions.description: The yacht named after Naomi sinks in a storm — Jordan's arrogant refusal to change course despite warnings mirrors his entire trajectory
Naomi files for divorce and full custody after Jordan endangers their daughter during a drug-fueled driving incident
Initially agrees to step down from Stratton to cooperate with the FBI, then reverses his decision in a manic speech, choosing his empire over his freedom

moments.label: The Arrest and Betrayal
moments.description: The FBI closes in. Jordan is arrested. Facing decades in prison, he agrees to wear a wire and inform on his colleagues — including Donnie. He passes Donnie a note warning him, but Denham intercepts it. Jordan's cooperation reduces his sentence but completes his moral collapse: the man who inspired fanatical loyalty betrays every person who trusted him. He is sentenced to 36 months in federal prison. Naomi is gone. Stratton Oakmont is shuttered. The money is seized. Every pillar of his identity is demolished.

character_states.char_jordan: broken, cooperating with the FBI, betraying everyone who followed him
character_states.char_denham: victorious, having dismantled the empire through patience and incorruptibility
character_states.char_donnie: betrayed by the man he worshipped, facing his own charges
character_states.char_max: devastated by his son's choices but relieved the charade is over

transitions.target: everything — wife, children, fortune, freedom, loyalty
transitions.description: Stripped of every trapping of his former life: Naomi divorces him, assets are seized, Stratton is closed, and he enters federal custody
Agrees to wear a wire against his closest ally, then tries to warn him but is caught — completing the destruction of every relationship he built
Secures the conviction he pursued for years, vindicating his refusal to be bought

moments.label: The Seminar Circuit
moments.description: After serving 22 months of a reduced sentence, Jordan Belfort reinvents himself as a motivational speaker and sales trainer. The final scene shows him at a seminar in Auckland, New Zealand, asking audience members to sell him a pen — the same exercise he used to recruit brokers at Stratton Oakmont. The audience stares at him with hungry, admiring eyes. The cycle is ready to begin again. Jordan has lost his empire but kept his one true asset: the ability to sell anything to anyone. The system that created him remains intact.

character_states.char_jordan: diminished but unbroken, recycling his charisma into a smaller arena, fundamentally unchanged

transitions.description: Reinvents himself from convicted felon into motivational speaker, but the transformation is superficial — the same salesmanship, the same hunger, the same audience of marks
The final 'sell me this pen' moment reveals that Jordan learned nothing — and that the world is always ready to be sold to again
